story:
Every night The Monster drank his poison, and I braced for the battle I knew was coming. In my small mind I ran to blue water and warm sand, to games of tag with my siblings, to the brief nights when my mother fled into the dark and, for a moment, we were free. I wanted a normal kind of love, not the twisted version that ruled my life. I was just a little girl. I did nothing to deserve it. Still, he made sure I heard it over and over: "This is your fault. You're just a stepchild."

The pattern never changed. Music blared. The door creaked open. He dragged me from bed by my ankles as my siblings and I clung to one another, until he yelled and they let go. I used to freeze, go somewhere else in my head, and wait for it to end. But the night that changed everything, I had a plan.

I had waited eleven years for a hero to show up. At thirteen, I finally accepted that nobody was coming. It was me or nothing. Being slapped earlier for not keeping the other kids quiet lit a fire I couldn't put out. When the knob turned, my body sparked into fight or flight—this time I chose fight. As he bent toward me, I gripped my brother's metal bat and swung hard at the side of his head. He was big, and he was drunk. He staggered, blood starting to flow. I swung again. He went down. Fear rushed in, but I knew I couldn't leave him to turn on my siblings or keep destroying us. I dropped the bat, scooped up the baby, and told the other two to follow. We ran into the cold, dark and found a neighbor.

She opened the door to four shivering kids. She looked at us with kindness, asked how she could help, and I choked on the words The Monster had fed me: "This is all my fault." Still, beneath the shock and the blood, there was a new kind of quiet in my head. Maybe this was what safety might feel like one day.

The police didn't arrest me. I was thirteen, and it was clearly self-defense. They already knew what our house held. The older officer from that night knelt beside my chair and looked me in the eyes. "This is not your fault," he said. "You were put in a situation no child should face. You protected your siblings. He can't hurt anyone ever again."

When it was time to speak in court, I stood. It was a custody hearing, not a criminal trial—the state needed to decide where we would go. The words "It's my fault" no longer fit in my mouth. I told the truth: how long the abuse had lasted, what he did, how he used "stepchild" as an excuse, and why I had to save us. The room went still. The judge placed us with our aunt, and I was ordered to therapy.

Many scars remain, mostly inside. Time softens them but doesn't erase them. I was once a terrified child. Now I am a survivor who saved herself. I tell my story for the little girls and boys still trapped in the dark, and for the grown women who still haven't spoken. Shame won't hold me anymore. The truth will.
